mn7:1.2: At one time the *Bhagavā* was dwelling at Sāvatthī, in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s park.
mn7:10.1: ‘I am endowed with verified confidence in the *saṅgha*’—he gains joy in the meaning, he gains joy in the *dhamma*, he gains gladness connected with the *dhamma*;
mn7:10.2: for one who is gladdened, *pīti* arises; for one whose mind is filled with *pīti*, the body becomes tranquil; one whose body is tranquil feels *sukha*; for one who feels *sukha*, the mind [*citta*] settles into *samādhi*.
mn7:11.1: ‘And insofar as this has been cast off, vomited out, released, abandoned, relinquished by me’—he gains joy in the meaning, he gains joy in the *dhamma*, he gains gladness connected with the *dhamma*;
mn7:11.2: for one who is gladdened, *pīti* arises; for one whose mind is filled with *pīti*, the body becomes tranquil; one whose body is tranquil feels *sukha*; for one who feels *sukha*, the mind [*citta*] settles into *samādhi*.
mn7:12.1: That *bhikkhu*, *bhikkhus*, being thus virtuous, of such *dhamma*, and of such discernment [*paññā*], even if he eats almsfood consisting of fine rice, with various sauces and curries, that is no obstacle for him.
mn7:12.2: Just as, *bhikkhus*, a cloth, soiled and stained with dirt, on coming to clear water becomes pure and bright; or gold, on coming into a furnace, becomes pure and bright;
mn7:12.3: in the same way, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu*, being thus virtuous, of such *dhamma*, and of such discernment [*paññā*], even if he eats almsfood consisting of fine rice, with various sauces and curries, that is no obstacle for him.
mn7:13.1: He dwells pervading one direction with a mind accompanied by goodwill [*mettā*], likewise the second, likewise the third, likewise the fourth. So above, below, across, everywhere, and in every respect, he dwells pervading the whole world with a mind accompanied by goodwill [*mettā*], abundant, expansive, immeasurable, free from enmity, free from ill will;
mn7:14.1: with a mind accompanied by compassion [*karuṇā*] …
mn7:15.1: with a mind accompanied by gladness [*muditā*] …
mn7:16.1: with a mind accompanied by equanimity [*upekkhā*] he dwells pervading one direction, likewise the second, likewise the third, likewise the fourth. So above, below, across, everywhere, and in every respect, he dwells pervading the whole world with a mind accompanied by equanimity [*upekkhā*], abundant, expansive, immeasurable, free from enmity, free from ill will.
mn7:17.1: He understands: ‘There is this, there is what is inferior, there is what is refined, there is an escape beyond this state of *saññā*.’
mn7:18.1: For one knowing thus and seeing thus, the mind [*citta*] is liberated from the *āsava* of sensuality [*kāmāsavā*], the mind [*citta*] is liberated from the *āsava* of *bhava* [*bhavāsavā*], the mind [*citta*] is liberated from the *āsava* of blindness [*avijjāsavā*].
mn7:18.2: When it is liberated, there is the knowledge, ‘It is liberated.’
mn7:18.3: He understands: ‘Birth is ended, the holy life has been lived, what had to be done has been done, there is no more of this state of being.’
mn7:19.1: Now at that time the brahmin Sundarikabhāradvāja was sitting not far from the *Bhagavā*.
mn7:19.2: Then the brahmin Sundarikabhāradvāja said this to the *Bhagavā*:
mn7:19.3: “Does Master Gotama go to the Bāhukā river to bathe?”
mn7:19.5: What will the Bāhukā river do?”
mn7:19.6: “For the Bāhukā river, Master Gotama, is acknowledged by many people, the Bāhukā river, Master Gotama, is regarded by many people as meritorious, and many people wash away bad deeds done in the Bāhukā river.”
mn7:2.1: “Just as, *bhikkhus*, a cloth is soiled and stained with dirt;
mn7:2.10: In the same way, *bhikkhus*, when the mind [*citta*] is undefiled, a good destination is to be expected.
mn7:2.2: if a dyer were to dip it in any dye—whether blue, yellow, red, or crimson—its color would turn out bad and impure.
mn7:2.5: In the same way, *bhikkhus*, when the mind [*citta*] is defiled, a bad destination is to be expected.
mn7:2.6: Just as, *bhikkhus*, a cloth is pure and bright;
mn7:2.7: if a dyer were to dip it in any dye—whether blue, yellow, red, or crimson—its color would turn out good and pure.
mn7:20.1: Then the *Bhagavā* addressed the brahmin Sundarikabhāradvāja in verses:
mn7:20.10: A hostile man, one who has done evil,
mn7:20.11: that evildoer is not cleansed by it.
mn7:20.12: For one who is pure, every day is a festival,
mn7:20.13: for one who is pure, every day is an observance day;
mn7:20.14: for one who is pure, whose actions are clean,
mn7:20.18: If you do not speak falsely,
mn7:20.19: if you do not harm a living being;
mn7:20.20: if you do not take what is not given,
mn7:20.22: what will you do by going to Gaya?
mn7:20.23: Even a well is Gaya for you.”
mn7:20.3: and Gaya, and also the Sundarikā;
mn7:20.6: though a fool may plunge in all the time,
mn7:20.7: one of dark deeds is not purified.
mn7:20.9: What can Payāga do, or the Bāhukā river?
mn7:21.1: When this was said, the brahmin Sundarikabhāradvāja said this to the *Bhagavā*:
mn7:21.3: Just as if someone were to set upright what had been overturned, or reveal what was concealed, or point out the way to one who was lost, or hold up an oil lamp in the dark so that those with eyes could see forms, so too Master Gotama has made the *dhamma* clear in many ways.
mn7:21.4: I go for refuge to Master Gotama, and to the *dhamma*, and to the *saṅgha* of *bhikkhus* [*bhikkhusaṅghañca*].
mn7:21.5: May I receive the going forth in the presence of Master Gotama, may I receive full ordination.”
mn7:22.1: And the brahmin Sundarikabhāradvāja received the going forth in the presence of the *Bhagavā*; he received full ordination.
mn7:22.2: Not long after his full ordination, the venerable Bhāradvāja, dwelling alone, withdrawn, diligent, ardent, and resolute, before long realized for himself with direct knowledge, in this very life, the unsurpassed culmination of the holy life for the sake of which clansmen rightly go forth from the house-life to homelessness, and having attained it, he dwelt.
mn7:22.3: He directly knew: ‘Birth is ended, the holy life has been lived, what had to be done has been done, there is no more of this state of being.’
mn7:22.4: And the venerable Bhāradvāja became one of the *arahants*.
mn7:22.5: The seventh, The Cloth, is finished.
mn7:3.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, are the defilements of the mind [*citta*]?
mn7:3.2: Covetousness together with unrighteous greed is a defilement of the mind [*citta*], ill will is a defilement of the mind [*citta*], anger is a defilement of the mind [*citta*], resentment is a defilement of the mind [*citta*], denigration is a defilement of the mind [*citta*], insolence is a defilement of the mind [*citta*], envy is a defilement of the mind [*citta*], stinginess is a defilement of the mind [*citta*], deceit is a defilement of the mind [*citta*], craftiness is a defilement of the mind [*citta*], stubbornness is a defilement of the mind [*citta*], aggression is a defilement of the mind [*citta*], conceit is a defilement of the mind [*citta*], arrogance is a defilement of the mind [*citta*], intoxication is a defilement of the mind [*citta*], negligence is a defilement of the mind [*citta*].
mn7:4.1: That *bhikkhu*, *bhikkhus*, having understood, ‘Covetousness together with unrighteous greed is a defilement of the mind [*citta*],’ abandons that defilement of the mind [*citta*] that is covetousness together with unrighteous greed;
mn7:4.10: ‘Insolence is a defilement of the mind [*citta*]’—
mn7:4.11: having understood this, he abandons that defilement of the mind [*citta*] that is insolence;
mn7:4.12: ‘Envy is a defilement of the mind [*citta*]’—
mn7:4.13: having understood this, he abandons that defilement of the mind [*citta*] that is envy;
mn7:4.14: ‘Stinginess is a defilement of the mind [*citta*]’—
mn7:4.15: having understood this, he abandons that defilement of the mind [*citta*] that is stinginess;
mn7:4.16: ‘Deceit is a defilement of the mind [*citta*]’—
mn7:4.17: having understood this, he abandons that defilement of the mind [*citta*] that is deceit;
mn7:4.18: ‘Craftiness is a defilement of the mind [*citta*]’—
mn7:4.19: having understood this, he abandons that defilement of the mind [*citta*] that is craftiness;
mn7:4.2: ‘Ill will is a defilement of the mind [*citta*]’—
mn7:4.20: ‘Stubbornness is a defilement of the mind [*citta*]’—
mn7:4.21: having understood this, he abandons that defilement of the mind [*citta*] that is stubbornness;
mn7:4.22: ‘Aggression is a defilement of the mind [*citta*]’—
mn7:4.23: having understood this, he abandons that defilement of the mind [*citta*] that is aggression;
mn7:4.24: ‘Conceit is a defilement of the mind [*citta*]’—
mn7:4.25: having understood this, he abandons that defilement of the mind [*citta*] that is conceit;
mn7:4.26: ‘Arrogance is a defilement of the mind [*citta*]’—
mn7:4.27: having understood this, he abandons that defilement of the mind [*citta*] that is arrogance;
mn7:4.28: ‘Intoxication is a defilement of the mind [*citta*]’—
mn7:4.29: having understood this, he abandons that defilement of the mind [*citta*] that is intoxication;
mn7:4.3: having understood this, he abandons that defilement of the mind [*citta*] that is ill will;
mn7:4.30: ‘Negligence is a defilement of the mind [*citta*]’—
mn7:4.31: having understood this, he abandons that defilement of the mind [*citta*] that is negligence.
mn7:4.4: ‘Anger is a defilement of the mind [*citta*]’—
mn7:4.5: having understood this, he abandons that defilement of the mind [*citta*] that is anger;
mn7:4.6: ‘Resentment is a defilement of the mind [*citta*]’—
mn7:4.7: having understood this, he abandons that defilement of the mind [*citta*] that is resentment;
mn7:4.8: ‘Denigration is a defilement of the mind [*citta*]’—
mn7:4.9: having understood this, he abandons that defilement of the mind [*citta*] that is denigration;
mn7:5.1: When, *bhikkhus*, for a *bhikkhu*, having understood, ‘Covetousness together with unrighteous greed is a defilement of the mind [*citta*],’ that defilement of the mind [*citta*] that is covetousness together with unrighteous greed has been abandoned,
mn7:5.10: ‘Insolence is a defilement of the mind [*citta*]’—
mn7:5.11: having understood this, that defilement of the mind [*citta*] that is insolence has been abandoned;
mn7:5.12: ‘Envy is a defilement of the mind [*citta*]’—
mn7:5.13: having understood this, that defilement of the mind [*citta*] that is envy has been abandoned;
mn7:5.14: ‘Stinginess is a defilement of the mind [*citta*]’—
mn7:5.15: having understood this, that defilement of the mind [*citta*] that is stinginess has been abandoned;
mn7:5.16: ‘Deceit is a defilement of the mind [*citta*]’—
mn7:5.17: having understood this, that defilement of the mind [*citta*] that is deceit has been abandoned;
mn7:5.18: ‘Craftiness is a defilement of the mind [*citta*]’—
mn7:5.19: having understood this, that defilement of the mind [*citta*] that is craftiness has been abandoned;
mn7:5.2: ‘Ill will is a defilement of the mind [*citta*]’—
mn7:5.20: ‘Stubbornness is a defilement of the mind [*citta*]’—
mn7:5.21: having understood this, that defilement of the mind [*citta*] that is stubbornness has been abandoned;
mn7:5.22: ‘Aggression is a defilement of the mind [*citta*]’—
mn7:5.23: having understood this, that defilement of the mind [*citta*] that is aggression has been abandoned;
mn7:5.24: ‘Conceit is a defilement of the mind [*citta*]’—
mn7:5.25: having understood this, that defilement of the mind [*citta*] that is conceit has been abandoned;
mn7:5.26: ‘Arrogance is a defilement of the mind [*citta*]’—
mn7:5.27: having understood this, that defilement of the mind [*citta*] that is arrogance has been abandoned;
mn7:5.28: ‘Intoxication is a defilement of the mind [*citta*]’—
mn7:5.29: having understood this, that defilement of the mind [*citta*] that is intoxication has been abandoned;
mn7:5.3: having understood this, that defilement of the mind [*citta*] that is ill will has been abandoned;
mn7:5.30: ‘Negligence is a defilement of the mind [*citta*]’—
mn7:5.31: having understood this, that defilement of the mind [*citta*] that is negligence has been abandoned.
mn7:5.32: He is endowed with verified confidence in the Buddha:
mn7:5.33: ‘Thus indeed is the *Bhagavā*: an arahant, perfectly awakened, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, gone to a good destination, knower of the world, unsurpassed guide of persons to be tamed, teacher of devatās and humans, awakened, the *Bhagavā*.’
mn7:5.4: ‘Anger is a defilement of the mind [*citta*]’—
mn7:5.5: having understood this, that defilement of the mind [*citta*] that is anger has been abandoned;
mn7:5.6: ‘Resentment is a defilement of the mind [*citta*]’—
mn7:5.7: having understood this, that defilement of the mind [*citta*] that is resentment has been abandoned;
mn7:5.8: ‘Denigration is a defilement of the mind [*citta*]’—
mn7:5.9: having understood this, that defilement of the mind [*citta*] that is denigration has been abandoned;
mn7:6.1: He is endowed with verified confidence in the *dhamma*:
mn7:6.2: ‘The *dhamma* has been well expounded by the *Bhagavā*, directly visible, immediate, inviting inspection, leading onward, to be experienced individually by the wise.’
mn7:7.1: He is endowed with verified confidence in the *saṅgha*:
mn7:7.2: ‘The disciple *saṅgha* [*sāvakasaṅgho*] of the *Bhagavā* has practiced well, the disciple *saṅgha* [*sāvakasaṅgho*] of the *Bhagavā* has practiced straightforwardly, the disciple *saṅgha* [*sāvakasaṅgho*] of the *Bhagavā* has practiced methodically, the disciple *saṅgha* [*sāvakasaṅgho*] of the *Bhagavā* has practiced properly; that is, the four pairs of persons, the eight kinds of persons. This disciple *saṅgha* [*sāvakasaṅgho*] of the *Bhagavā* is worthy of gifts, worthy of hospitality, worthy of offerings, worthy of reverential salutation, an unsurpassed field of merit for the world.’
mn7:8.1: And insofar as this has been cast off, vomited out, released, abandoned, relinquished by him, he gains joy in the meaning, he gains joy in the *dhamma*, he gains gladness connected with the *dhamma*.
mn7:8.2: For one who is gladdened, *pīti* arises; for one whose mind is filled with *pīti*, the body becomes tranquil; one whose body is tranquil feels *sukha*; for one who feels *sukha*, the mind [*citta*] settles into *samādhi*;
mn7:9.1: ‘I am endowed with verified confidence in the *dhamma*’ …
